transcript.transcript: Hi, my name is Thad Webster. I'm a participant in the American 250 in Idaho Applied Oral History Project. Today is March 13th, 2026. I'm speaking with Thornton Waite. We'll hear about Thornton's experiences researching and publishing Idaho's train history. This oral history is conducted with the support of the Idaho State Historical Society and TheirStory as a part of the America 250 and Idaho commemoration. The issue supports the commemorations mission to advance the ideals of the American Revolution, equality, liberty and justice through meaningful community engagement. This project seeks to recognize, appreciate and commemorate Idaho's journey in America's legacy. Thornton. Firstly, let me say thank you so much for your time today to share your thoughts and memories about this topic. This interview will allow you to reflect on and share what you've learned about Idaho's train history. Through these questions, I hope to gain insight into the role trains played in Idaho's development and growth. I'll begin the interview with some general questions to start the conversation, and we can get into more specific topics from there. During the interview, I may adjust the order of some of the questions to allow for a natural flow of topics, and I might ask unscripted follow up questions to clarify your answers. Before we jump into the questions, do you give permission to record this interview? Yes, I do give permission. Can I have you state and spell your first and last name? My name is Thornton Waite. T h o r n t o n w a I t e. Thank you very much. We're going to go through a timeline, essentially kind of like the old TV story that you. I remember, This Is Your Life. Yeah. Remember that TV story? But we're not going to have people coming out giving their own testimonials of your life. You'll have to do that yourself. Okay. Uh, first question, what was your earliest memory of seeing a train in operation or a train depot? I grew up back east in Connecticut. My first memory of a train was when my mother took me down to the railroad station and to pick up my father. He'd, uh, go on a business trip in New York City, and we'd go down and pick him up at the end of the day, when he'd. After his day, day in the city. It was not a very common occurrence, but it was just one of those special events to see my father. And about what age were you? I, I was probably 5 or 6 in that, in that range. He would go down there. I lived in Hartford and he would go down for a day business trip and mom would take him down in the morning and we'd. I remember going to the station and pick him up. And in the early evening. What was your impression of trains at that time? If you even. They were big. They were noisy. I mean, they were big. And, the railroad the New Haven Railroad, was not in good condition. So everything was kind of dirty and dingy. But it was it was just kind of exciting to hear and see the train. And was it a big station or just a? Hartford station was very big and, far bigger than it needed to be. So again, it was, it was old, it was worn out. And, I just remember it was always too hot because it was a huge, huge waiting room. Oh yeah. I just remembered parking and walking under the tracks and you'd hear the train going over, going over us, and then we'd walk up to the platform. So in your youth there are two, there are two sets of experiences that start to track each other. And that is growing up and going through the usual education toward the profession. And then that is exploring trains as a subject, you know. As a hobby. Yes. Research about and so on and so forth. So what was your education path. I went to public high public schools through high school. Then I went to an engineering school, got my degree in mechanical engineering. Then I went to graduate school and got my degree in nuclear engineering. And then I worked at the Idaho National Laboratory. Most of my career. Out here in Idaho? In Idaho. Yes. So along that line, you know, nuclear engineering is not common to most of us people. Yeah. It, it's,sort of listened to in amazement when someone says it. And then you imagine what on earth do they do in nuclear engineering? But, in your case, I'm interested in when did you know that that's the profession you wanted to follow? When I was a freshman in college. I went to University of Maine and I was, you know, got the introductory engineering courses. And, my father was in the nuclear industry, coincidentally, I didn't feel any pressure. Oh, but I was, I was looking into it and it appealed to me. So I transferred to an engineering school, Worcester Polytechnic Institute. And because they had a program for nuclear engineers and I combined the mechanical engineering, sort of the practical aspect with the nuclear engineering. And it was a good combination. Okay. Interesting. And then. During your growing up in education, junior high, high school, etc., did you ever start writing about trains before you got to Idaho? When I was in high school, I, well, I had a model railroad layout naturally, and my parents pretty well encouraged me on that. And then When I wrote a couple papers in high school on trains because I was interested in them. And, I found it interesting just to read about the history of these railroads, because I'd seen all these abandoned railroad lines in the state and I'd researched them and find out, learned about it. And so I wrote that type of paper. I kept taking pictures. Then I went to college, and I really didn't have a lot of time to think about railroad history. It wasn't until I started working that I had some spare time to. Yeah. Do do more detailed research and the capabilities. Yeah. And so, do you consider you do this as a profession, as in you have an interest and you like getting paid for it? No, I do not at all. It's strictly a hobby. I once in a while I write these short articles for Model Railroading magazine and they give me a you could almost call it a small honorarium for doing it. This is just more of just an interest in learning more about the trains every time I do some research. If something else comes up and I just. There's always more to find, learn and find out. So what would you consider to be your first. Impressive deliverable publication? I was writing articles after college. I wrote articles for a couple of magazines, and then somehow I got together with a friend and we put out a book about the railroad from Idaho Falls up to West Yellowstone. It was kind of fit into what I was learning about research in eastern Idaho. And I had information so that was really my first book, Yellowstone Branch of the Union Pacific. Okay. And after that, once, once I got one book, there was always more information. And I kept reading and researching more. So I've been able to write several books since then. So that first book will say, I mean, there could have been obviously there were articles or papers in school, and there may have been another sort of organized production of something before that, but we'll just take that as a starting point. Yeah. The West Yellowstone book you've written nearly, and there have been published that I can find, nearly 20 books. It's about 16. Yes. Okay. And, and then obviously a lot of articles that go into various journals and stuff. When you now having written and published 16 books, when you look back on that first book, what's your assessment of that book from the standpoint of what you kind of imagined wanting to produce? Was it 50% of what you thought and you needed to do more work or something? Was it like 90% what you thought. On the first book it covered what I wanted to cover the history of the line, but then it kind of led me into, well, I have this additional information about another railroad line, and they were tied together. You know, historically. And so it kind of, I guess you could say you pull the thread on one and it leads you to another one, which leads you to yet another one. And there's always, as I said, there's always more, more history. And, to be honest, with the internet, there's a lot more information. So, it, it just keeps ballooning really. It's varied over all the books I've written. I've done it several different ways. Yeah, yeah. Again, the audience changes. You have to look at the audience. So you have to figure out how to let people know about books and stuff. So a comment you made there leads to another interesting point there. Well, I'm real thankful for everything that you've been able to share with us on this little tour through Idaho trains and their effect on prosperity and growth. Well, remember, Boise was not on the main line, and as soon as the railroad was built through Nampa and Caldwell in 1881 or 82, Boise said, we want to be on the main line. And it wasn't until 1925, well, just over 100 years ago, that they were on the main line. But and it was extremely important for them at the time. Yeah, yeah. You didn't want to ride this little podunk local train to get to the capital city you want? Yeah. They wanted the main line trains to go go through and stop at Boise. So it was important. It was a big deal when the train came. Yes. Which is why they kept pressing 40 years. Yes, exactly. It got there. It did. Okay. Well, I hope I talked to you again, Thornton. Yes. Me too. Thanks. Thank you.

lines.line: # Introduction and Interview Setup

> The interviewer introduces himself, the oral history project, and its purpose, highlighting the commemoration of Idaho's journey in America's legacy. He explains the interview format, seeks permission to record, and asks the interviewee to spell his name. This section establishes the context and structure for the conversation.

metadata.title: Introduction and Interview Setup
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer introduces himself, the oral history project, and its purpose, highlighting the commemoration of Idaho's journey in America's legacy. He explains the interview format, seeks permission to record, and asks the interviewee to spell his name. This section establishes the context and structure for the conversation.

lines.line: # Early Memories of Trains

> The interviewee recalls his childhood in Connecticut, describing his first experiences with trains when his mother took him to the station to pick up his father. He shares impressions of the trains and the station, noting their size, noise, and the worn condition of the facilities. These early memories set the stage for his lifelong interest in trains.

metadata.title: Early Memories of Trains
metadata.synopsis: The interviewee recalls his childhood in Connecticut, describing his first experiences with trains when his mother took him to the station to pick up his father. He shares impressions of the trains and the station, noting their size, noise, and the worn condition of the facilities. These early memories set the stage for his lifelong interest in trains.

lines.line: # Education and Professional Path

> The speaker outlines his educational journey, from public school through college, where he pursued mechanical and nuclear engineering. He explains how his father's involvement in the nuclear industry influenced his decision, and describes his career at the Idaho National Laboratory. This section provides background on his professional expertise and its connection to his later research interests.

metadata.title: Education and Professional Path
metadata.synopsis: The speaker outlines his educational journey, from public school through college, where he pursued mechanical and nuclear engineering. He explains how his father's involvement in the nuclear industry influenced his decision, and describes his career at the Idaho National Laboratory. This section provides background on his professional expertise and its connection to his later research interests.

lines.line: # Early Writing and Train Research

> The interviewee discusses his early engagement with trains, including building model railroads and writing school papers on railroad history. He describes how his curiosity about abandoned lines led to research and photography, and notes that his interest in railroad history deepened once he began working and had more time for detailed study.

metadata.title: Early Writing and Train Research
metadata.synopsis: The interviewee discusses his early engagement with trains, including building model railroads and writing school papers on railroad history. He describes how his curiosity about abandoned lines led to research and photography, and notes that his interest in railroad history deepened once he began working and had more time for detailed study.

lines.line: # First Book and Publishing Experience

> The speaker recounts his transition from writing articles to publishing his first book about the Yellowstone Branch of the Union Pacific. He explains how this project emerged from his research in eastern Idaho and led to further books and articles. The process of publishing and the discovery of interconnected railroad histories are highlighted as key motivators for continued research.

metadata.title: First Book and Publishing Experience
metadata.synopsis: The speaker recounts his transition from writing articles to publishing his first book about the Yellowstone Branch of the Union Pacific. He explains how this project emerged from his research in eastern Idaho and led to further books and articles. The process of publishing and the discovery of interconnected railroad histories are highlighted as key motivators for continued research.

lines.line: # Evolution of Writing and Research Process

> The interviewee reflects on how his writing and research methods have evolved, emphasizing the impact of technological advancements such as computers and the internet. He describes improvements in organizing information, preparing images, and streamlining the production process. Experience has made writing easier, and he has developed a consistent format for his books.

metadata.title: Evolution of Writing and Research Process
metadata.synopsis: The interviewee reflects on how his writing and research methods have evolved, emphasizing the impact of technological advancements such as computers and the internet. He describes improvements in organizing information, preparing images, and streamlining the production process. Experience has made writing easier, and he has developed a consistent format for his books.

lines.line: # Audience and Distribution Changes

> The speaker discusses shifts in the audience for railroad books, noting a decline in book sales and changing distribution channels. He observes that younger generations are less likely to buy books, and that railroad hobby stores have largely disappeared. Despite a smaller audience, specialized groups remain interested, and the speaker adapts his writing and publishing strategies accordingly.

metadata.title: Audience and Distribution Changes
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses shifts in the audience for railroad books, noting a decline in book sales and changing distribution channels. He observes that younger generations are less likely to buy books, and that railroad hobby stores have largely disappeared. Despite a smaller audience, specialized groups remain interested, and the speaker adapts his writing and publishing strategies accordingly.

lines.line: # Differences Between Eastern and Western Railroads

> The interviewee describes the stark contrasts between railroads in the East and those in Idaho, including differences in track conditions, employee morale, and types of trains. He notes that eastern railroads were often bankrupt and deteriorating, while Union Pacific in Idaho was profitable and well-maintained. This shift inspired him to research Idaho's railroads and fill gaps in the historical record.

metadata.title: Differences Between Eastern and Western Railroads
metadata.synopsis: The interviewee describes the stark contrasts between railroads in the East and those in Idaho, including differences in track conditions, employee morale, and types of trains. He notes that eastern railroads were often bankrupt and deteriorating, while Union Pacific in Idaho was profitable and well-maintained. This shift inspired him to research Idaho's railroads and fill gaps in the historical record.

lines.line: # Role of Railroads in Idaho's Development

> The conversation explores how Union Pacific and other railroads facilitated Idaho's settlement and economic growth. The speaker explains that railroads were essential for developing southern and northern Idaho, enabling agriculture, mining, and transportation of goods. The deliberate construction of branch lines attracted settlers and made prosperity possible, with trains playing a central role in moving crops and supplies.

metadata.title: Role of Railroads in Idaho's Development
metadata.synopsis: The conversation explores how Union Pacific and other railroads facilitated Idaho's settlement and economic growth. The speaker explains that railroads were essential for developing southern and northern Idaho, enabling agriculture, mining, and transportation of goods. The deliberate construction of branch lines attracted settlers and made prosperity possible, with trains playing a central role in moving crops and supplies.

lines.line: # Subtopics in Train History: Passenger Trains, Stations, Architecture

> The interviewee discusses various subtopics within train history, such as passenger trains, train stations, and architectural design. He explains how research into specific lines often leads to discoveries about station architecture and the work of notable architects. The importance of stations in town development and the interconnectedness of passenger services are highlighted, along with the speaker's contributions to specialized publications.

metadata.title: Subtopics in Train History: Passenger Trains, Stations, Architecture
metadata.synopsis: The interviewee discusses various subtopics within train history, such as passenger trains, train stations, and architectural design. He explains how research into specific lines often leads to discoveries about station architecture and the work of notable architects. The importance of stations in town development and the interconnectedness of passenger services are highlighted, along with the speaker's contributions to specialized publications.

lines.line: # Assessment of Union Pacific and Railroad Operations

> The interviewer asks for an evaluation of Union Pacific's performance and business practices. The speaker acknowledges the company's success and the challenges it faced, including criticism over rates and the complexity of managing a vast network. He emphasizes the importance of capital investment, coordination, and logistical expertise in delivering reliable service and supporting Idaho's growth.

metadata.title: Assessment of Union Pacific and Railroad Operations
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer asks for an evaluation of Union Pacific's performance and business practices. The speaker acknowledges the company's success and the challenges it faced, including criticism over rates and the complexity of managing a vast network. He emphasizes the importance of capital investment, coordination, and logistical expertise in delivering reliable service and supporting Idaho's growth.

lines.line: # Engineering, Technology, and Management in Railroad Construction

> The discussion delves into the technological and managerial achievements of railroad companies, especially Union Pacific. The speaker describes how early railroads relied on engineering expertise, adapted eastern practices to western challenges, and innovated in surveying, bridge construction, and locomotive design. The influence of military culture and the role of educated leaders are noted as crucial factors in overcoming logistical and technical obstacles.

metadata.title: Engineering, Technology, and Management in Railroad Construction
metadata.synopsis: The discussion delves into the technological and managerial achievements of railroad companies, especially Union Pacific. The speaker describes how early railroads relied on engineering expertise, adapted eastern practices to western challenges, and innovated in surveying, bridge construction, and locomotive design. The influence of military culture and the role of educated leaders are noted as crucial factors in overcoming logistical and technical obstacles.

lines.line: # Remnants and Legacy of Idaho Railroads

> The interviewee reflects on the enduring legacy of Idaho's railroads, pointing out rail trails, repurposed bridges, and the remnants of infrastructure in towns. He describes how old railroad features, such as grain elevators and stockyards, continue to shape local landscapes and serve as reminders of the railroads' historical importance. The adaptability and thriftiness of railroad companies are illustrated through examples of reused materials.

metadata.title: Remnants and Legacy of Idaho Railroads
metadata.synopsis: The interviewee reflects on the enduring legacy of Idaho's railroads, pointing out rail trails, repurposed bridges, and the remnants of infrastructure in towns. He describes how old railroad features, such as grain elevators and stockyards, continue to shape local landscapes and serve as reminders of the railroads' historical importance. The adaptability and thriftiness of railroad companies are illustrated through examples of reused materials.

lines.line: # Boise's Railroad History and Closing Remarks

> The interview concludes with a discussion of Boise's efforts to secure a place on the main railroad line, underscoring the significance of rail access for the city's development. The speaker notes the persistence of local leaders and the eventual success in 1925. Final remarks express gratitude and anticipation for future conversations, wrapping up the oral history interview.

story.title: Idaho Trains - Thornton Waite
story.description: In the interview, Thornton Waite, a historian and author, shares insights into Idaho's train history, emphasizing the Union Pacific Railroad's pivotal role in the state's development. Trains facilitated agricultural prosperity and community growth through infrastructure expansion, prompting settlement in less populated areas. Waite's journey from an interest in trains sparked by childhood experiences to a career at the Idaho National Laboratory reflects his unique blend of engineering and historical research. He highlights contributions by military veterans in railroad construction, showcasing the interconnection of military discipline with engineering prowess. Overall, Waite underscores the lasting impact of railroads on Idaho's economic and cultural landscape.
